Executive Summary

  • ✅ Best strategy for service: Conservative achieves the highest mean service level of 73.8% across all scenarios.
  • ❌ Crisis impact: Calm→Crisis reduces service level from 67.3% to 55.7% (Δ=11.6pp) and increases lost sales from €1.54M to €2.78M.
  • 🔗 Cost–Service trade-off: Correlation r = 0.586. Higher service levels come with higher total costs.
  • 📈 Distributed resilience: In crisis, service levels are 46.8% (Centralized), 62.3% (Distributed), 58.0% (Hybrid).

1. Design of Experiments

A full-factorial design explores how inventory strategy, network configuration, and disruption scenario interact to shape supply chain resilience.

Factors

FactorLevelsDescription
Inventory Strategy3Conservative (3wk safety), Balanced (2wk), Lean/JIT (1wk)
Network Config3Centralized (80% via 1 DC), Distributed (even), Hybrid (regional+overflow)
Disruption Scenario4Calm (2%), Moderate (5%), Volatile (10%), Crisis (20%)

Network Topology

NodeTypeKey Parameters
🌎 Asia-PrimarySupplierLead 4wk, Rel 92%, Cap 5k/wk, €8/unit
🇪🇺 Europe-SecondarySupplierLead 2wk, Rel 97%, Cap 3k/wk, €12/unit
🇺🇸 Americas-BackupSupplierLead 3wk, Rel 95%, Cap 2k/wk, €10/unit
🏭 Plant-East / WestManufacturingCap 4k–3.5k/wk, Yield 93–95%
📦 DC-North / Central / SouthDistributionStorage 15k–25k units, Holding €0.50/unit/wk
👤 5 Customer RegionsDemandSeasonal, Trending, Volatile, Stable, Growing

4. Key Insights

✅ Best strategy for service: Conservative achieves the highest mean service level of 73.8% across all scenarios.
❌ Crisis impact: Calm→Crisis reduces service level from 67.3% to 55.7% (Δ=11.6pp) and increases lost sales from €1.54M to €2.78M.
🔗 Cost–Service trade-off: Correlation r = 0.586. Higher service levels come with higher total costs.
📈 Distributed resilience: In crisis, service levels are 46.8% (Centralized), 62.3% (Distributed), 58.0% (Hybrid).
💨 Worst case: Lean/JIT under Crisis achieves only 38.8% service level with €3.43M lost sales.

5. Network Configuration Analysis

💡 Why Centralized Often Outperforms:
  1. Inventory pooling effect — a single large DC requires less safety stock than multiple small DCs serving the same total demand (√n pooling).
  2. Reduced lateral transshipments — with centralized inventory, demand variability across regions is averaged out.
  3. Lower total holding costs — fewer units needed system-wide.

However, centralized networks carry higher single-point-of-failure risk, which becomes apparent in Crisis scenarios where the central DC can become overwhelmed.

Network ConfigMean SL [%]Mean Cost [€M]Mean Fill Rate [%]Mean Lead Time [wk]Mean Backorder [%]
Centralized49.44.2362.06.182.7
Distributed68.25.8789.35.931.9
Hybrid67.25.8989.85.933.6
📊 Best network: Distributed delivers the highest average service level of 68.2%.

9. Methodology & Model Description

9.1 Architecture

Discrete-time supply chain simulation with weekly time steps over 52 weeks. Enhanced with bidirectional demand shocks (surge & recession), cross-DC transshipment, and incoming quality inspection (5% rejection). Self-contained engine running inside ProcessPoolExecutor for embarrassingly parallel Monte Carlo execution.

9.2 Network Model

3 Suppliers (Asia, Europe, Americas) with differentiated lead times, reliability, capacity and unit costs. 2 Manufacturing Plants with yield rates. 3 Distribution Centres with storage capacity limits. 5 Customer Regions with distinct demand patterns (seasonal, trending, volatile, stable, growing).

9.3 Disruption Model

Three disruption types: supplier failure (reduces capacity to 0–30% for 1–8 weeks), transport delay (adds 1–4 weeks), demand shock (multiplies demand by 1.5–3.0× for 2–6 weeks). Probabilities scaled by scenario from 2% (Calm) to 20% (Crisis) per week per node.

9.4 Monte Carlo Design

3 strategies × 3 networks × 4 disruption scenarios = 36 combinations × 400 reps = 14400 runs. 52 weeks each. Executed with 12 CPU cores in 10.6s.

10. Interactive Analysis

11. Strategic Insights by Persona

Data-driven recommendations derived from the simulation results, tailored for four key stakeholder groups.

01
🌐
Network Design Impact
Distributed network achieves 68.2% SL vs Centralized 49.4% and Hybrid 67.2%. Under crisis, distributed shows highest resilience at 62.3%.
02
🏆
Optimal Strategy Selection
Conservative delivers 73.8% SL at €5.72M cost. The cost premium over Lean is €0.83M.
03
📊
Service Level Optimisation
Best achievable SL (Conservative+Calm): 78.3%. Service–inventory correlation: r = 0.872. Safety stock is the primary lever.
04
📦
DC Capacity Planning
Mean inventory across all runs: 4.0k units. Inventory turnover: 141.8×/yr. Conservative strategy reduces turnover but improves fill rate to 84.5%.
05
✅
Strategic Recommendation
Deploy Conservative with Distributed network for optimal resilience. This combination yields 81.8% SL across all disruption scenarios.
01
🛡
Disruption Preparedness
Conservative strategy maintains 68.3% SL even in crisis, vs Lean at 38.8%. Safety stock is the best disruption insurance.
02
⏰
Recovery Metrics
Mean recovery time: Conservative 18.9 wk, Balanced 21.1 wk, Lean 24.3 wk. Faster recovery = less cumulative lost revenue.
03
💰
Financial Exposure
Cash flow at risk: Calm €1.84M vs Crisis €3.10M. Crisis increases exposure by 1.7×.
04
🌋
Worst-Case Scenarios
Lean + Centralized + Crisis: 31.2% SL, €5.30M lost. This is the riskiest combination.
05
🛠
Risk Mitigation
Distributed network acts as insurance: crisis SL is 15.4pp higher than centralized. The premium is €1.64M extra cost in calm times.
01
🌎
Supplier Reliability Impact
3-supplier network provides redundancy. Asia-Primary (92% rel, €8) vs Europe-Secondary (97% rel, €12). Mean disruption days: 316 across all scenarios.
02
💰
Cost Optimisation
Procurement: €1.95M, Manufacturing: €1.14M, Transport: €1.31M. Procurement is the largest cost driver at 36% of total.
03
🔄
Multi-Sourcing Benefits
Triple-sourcing means even when one supplier fails (up to 8 weeks), the other two can cover 139% of base demand. Cost is only 11.2 €/unit avg from backups.
04
⏲
Lead Time Management
Average lead time: 6.0 weeks. Europe-Secondary at 2wk provides fastest response; Asia-Primary at 4wk is cheapest. Lead time variability is the key driver of safety stock requirements.
05
📈
Inventory-Cost Trade-off
Conservative holds 6.9k units (holding: €0.18M) vs Lean 1.1k units (holding: €0.03M). But lost sales offset holding savings.
01
⚖️
Strategic Trade-offs
The cost-service Pareto frontier shows diminishing returns above 95% SL. Conservative costs €5.72M for 73.8% SL, while Lean saves €0.83M but drops to 46.1% SL.
02
💸
Investment Priorities
Investing in safety stock (Conservative → +€0.83M) yields +27.7pp SL. Investing in distributed network adds €1.64M but provides crisis resilience.
03
💪
Competitive Resilience
Under crisis conditions, our best configuration maintains 77.2% SL. Lean competitors would drop to 31.2%.
04
📈
Financial Summary
Mean annual supply chain cost: €5.33M. Mean annual lost sales: €2.11M. Perfect order rate: 61.6%. Total exposure: €2.43M.
05
🎯
Executive Recommendation
Deploy Conservative strategy with Distributed network. Accept the €5.72M annual cost for 73.8% service. This is the optimal point on the cost-resilience frontier.

💰 Hidden Cost: Lost sales represent €2.11M (28.4% of total landed cost) — the hidden cost not captured in traditional accounting. Lost sales are computed as backorder quantity × €50/unit sell price.

14. Bullwhip Effect Analysis

The bullwhip effect occurs when order variability amplifies as we move upstream from customer to supplier. A bullwhip ratio > 1 indicates order amplification; values near 1 indicate stable ordering.

3.36
Conservative Bullwhip Ratio
1.63
Balanced Bullwhip Ratio
0.32
Lean/JIT Bullwhip Ratio
📈 Bullwhip Insight: Conservative strategy (bullwhip ratio = 3.36) amplifies orders more than Lean/JIT (0.32). Higher safety stock absorbs demand variability, reducing order amplification upstream.

15. Cash-to-Cash Cycle Analysis

Cash-to-Cash (C2C) measures the time between paying suppliers and receiving payment from customers: C2C = DIO + DSO − DPO.

Component Definitions

ComponentFormulaValue
Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO)avg_inventory / daily_demandVaries by strategy
Days Payable Outstanding (DPO)Supplier payment terms30 days
Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)Customer collection period45 days

C2C by Strategy

27
Conservative [days]
22
Balanced [days]
17
Lean/JIT [days]
💰 Working Capital: Lean/JIT achieves 10.4 days lower C2C than Conservative, freeing approximately €0.30M in working capital.

🌎 KPI Glossary & Definitions

Detailed explanation of every metric used in this analysis, with industry benchmarks and authoritative sources to help all stakeholders interpret results.

KPIDefinitionWhy It MattersIndustry Benchmark
Service Level [%]Fraction of demand fulfilled on timeThe primary logistics performance indicator. Below 95% signals systematic issues.Class A: ≥95% (APICS/ASCM)
Fill Rate [%]Fraction of ordered quantity actually deliveredOrder completeness measure. Distinct from service level (which is binary per order).≥97% for Class A
Total Cost [€M]Holding + transport + procurement costsTotal supply chain operating cost. Trade-off with service level.Benchmark: 5–8% of revenue
Lead Time [wk]Order-to-delivery time including delaysDrives safety stock requirements: SS = z × σ × √LT.Simchi-Levi, 2008
Lost Sales [€M]Revenue lost due to stockoutsDirect revenue impact + customer lifetime value erosion. Hard to recover.Target: <2% of revenue
Backorder Rate [%]Fraction of demand backorderedSystematic backorders signal structural capacity/inventory issues.<5% for responsive supply chains
Avg InventoryMean units held across all DCsHolding cost driver. Balance against stockout risk.Optimize via EOQ models
Inv TurnoverAnnual demand / average inventoryHigher = leaner. But very high turnover increases stockout risk.8–12 manufacturing; 20+ retail
Disruption DaysTotal days of supply disruptionResilience metric. Geopolitical risk has increased this 3× since 2019.Ivanov et al., 2017
Recovery Time [wk]Weeks to restore full operations post-disruptionResilience speed. Distributed networks recover faster.Target: <4 weeks for critical paths
Cash at Risk [€M]Weighted cash flow exposure under disruptionCombines inventory value, lost sales, and emergency costs.Stress-test against 2× worst-case
Perfect Order Rate [%]Orders delivered complete, on-time, undamagedThe "holy grail" metric combining service level, fill rate, and quality.Best-in-class: ≥90%
Bullwhip RatioVar(DC orders) / Var(customer demand)Measures order amplification upstream. Values >1 indicate the bullwhip effect; higher values mean more volatility in supply chain.<2.0 is well-managed (Lee et al., 1997)
Cash-to-Cash [days]DIO + DSO − DPODays between paying suppliers and receiving customer payment. Lower C2C frees working capital and improves cash flow.Best-in-class: <30 days

📚 Sources & References

Industry standards, academic research, and data sources backing the model assumptions and benchmarks cited in this report.

#SourceUsed For
1APICS/ASCM — Supply Chain Operations Reference (SCOR)Service level, fill rate, and perfect order rate definitions and benchmarks
2Simchi-Levi, D. (2008). Designing and Managing the Supply ChainSafety stock formulas, lead-time variability, network design
3Lee, Padmanabhan & Whang (1997). Management ScienceBullwhip effect: demand signal amplification through supply chain tiers
4Ivanov, Sokolov & Dolgui (2017). IJPESupply chain resilience, disruption recovery, ripple effect
5Supply Chain QuarterlyInventory turnover benchmarks by industry
6Ho et al. (2015). EJORMulti-criteria supplier evaluation and lateral transshipment models
